title: Coconut ice
subtitle: Old-fashioned no-cook coconut sweet in pink and white layers
excerpt: A classic British no-cook sweet made from desiccated coconut, condensed milk, and icing sugar, set in a tin and cut into squares. Half is left white, half tinted pink. Makes about thirty pieces.
sourceNotes: Coconut ice is documented in British domestic confectionery from the late nineteenth century onward. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: coconut sweets made from desiccated coconut, sugar, and egg white are recorded in the confectionery chapter. The no-cook version using condensed milk (which replaces the egg white and provides both binding and sweetness) is documented in early twentieth-century British domestic cookbooks, where it became a standard school fête and home-made sweet preparation.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin at room temperature for up to 2 weeks.

content.text: Coconut ice sets by the sugar in the condensed milk and icing sugar crystallising around the coconut as the mixture cools. It needs no cooking, which makes it one of the simplest sweets in the British repertoire. The mixture is sticky and stiff; use your hands to press it firmly and evenly into the tin.

Line a 20 × 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Combine the {{desiccated-coconut}}, {{condensed-milk}}, and {{icing-sugar}} in a large bowl and mix until a stiff, cohesive paste forms. Knead briefly with your hands; the warmth helps it come together. Divide in half. Press one half firmly and evenly into the lined tin to form a layer about 1.5 cm thick. Work the {{food-colouring}} into the second half until the colour is even throughout, then press this pink layer on top of the white layer, pressing firmly to bond them. Smooth the surface. Cover and refrigerate for at least 3 hours until firmly set. Lift out using the paper and cut into squares with a sharp knife.
